message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  We model the time-evolution of the number
                  <jats:italic>N(t)</jats:italic>
                  of individuals
                  <jats:italic>reported</jats:italic>
                  to be infected in a given country with a specific virus, in terms of a Riccati equation. Although this equation is nonlinear and it contains time-dependent coefficients, it can be solved in closed form, yielding an expression for
                  <jats:italic>N(t)</jats:italic>
                  that depends on a function
                  <jats:italic>α(t)</jats:italic>
                  . For the particular case that
                  <jats:italic>α(t)</jats:italic>
                  is constant, this expression reduces to the well-known
                  <jats:italic>logistic formula</jats:italic>
                  , giving rise to a sigmoidal curve suitable for modelling usual epidemics. However, for the case of the COVID-19 pandemic, the long series of available data shows that the use of this simple formula for predictions underestimates
                  <jats:italic>N(t);</jats:italic>
                  thus, the logistic formula only provides a
                  <jats:italic>lower bound</jats:italic>
                  of
                  <jats:italic>N(t)</jats:italic>
                  . After experimenting with more than 50 different forms of
                  <jats:italic>α(t)</jats:italic>
                  , we introduce two novel models that will be referred to as “
                  <jats:italic>rational”</jats:italic>
                  and
                  <jats:italic>“birational”</jats:italic>
                  . The parameters specifying these models (as well as those of the logistic model), are determined from the available data using an error-minimizing algorithm. The analysis of the applicability of the above models to the cases of China and South Korea suggest that they yield more accurate predictions, and importantly that they may provide an
                  <jats:italic>upper bound</jats:italic>
                  of the actual
                  <jats:italic>N(t)</jats:italic>
                  . Results are presented for Italy, Spain, and France.
                </jats:p>
title: Predictive mathematical models for the number of individuals infected with COVID-19

reference.unstructured: Ferguson NM , Laydon D , Nedjati-Gilani G , Imai N , Ainslie K , Baguelin M , Bhatia S , Boonyasiri A ., Cucunubá Z , Cuomo-Dannenburg G ., et al. (2020). Impact of non-pharmaceutical interventions (NPIs) to reduce COVID-19 mortality and healthcare demand. London: Imperial College COVID-19 Response Team, March 16, 2020.
W. E. Schiesser . A Mathematical Modeling Approach to Infectious Diseases, Cross Diffusion PDE Models for Epidemiology (World Scientific Publishing Company, 2018).
Errico JD , (2005). Bound Constrained Optimization, Computer Program. MATLAB and Simulink Centre, The MathWorks, Inc.
Press WH , Teukolsky SA , Vetterling WT , Flannery BP (1994). Numerical Recipes in C. Cambridge university press, Cambridge, New York.
Moré JJ (1977). The Levenberg-Marquardt Algorithm: Implementation and Theory. Numerical Analysis, ed. G. A. Watson , Lecture Notes in Mathematics 630, Springer Verlag, pp. 105–116.
Liu T , Hu J , Kang M , Lin L , H. Zhong , Xiao J (2020. Transmission dynamics of 2019 novel coronavirus (2019-nCoV). BioRxiv.
Anastassopoulou C , Russo L , Tsakris A , Siettos C (2020). Data-based analysis, modelling and forecasting of the novel coronavirus (2019-nCoV) outbreak. medRxiv.
